WandbLogger is a PyTorch lightning module. I'll create an issue to their repo.

More context here:
For performance concern, wandb asynchronously uploads artifacts and automatically triggers `wandb.finish()`(as a barrier) when your script exits, so PyTorch Lightning don't do this explicitly.

In Ray, we kill actors right after training is complete, so wandb may not be able to upload all files before the actor dies. So we have to explicitly call `finish()`.

I honestly don't have a clear answer for it.. just taking a look at the code `on_connection_state_changed`, the chain of call is like
1. connection notifies its all subsidiary sessions
2. session notify its all subsidiary links that session state is changed
3. link gets notified and could perform
3.1 attach
3.2 detach and clean up deliveries

I think we should apply single responsibility principle here -- connection module only deals with connection error, error that doesn't belong to this module, leave that error to upstream/downstream to handle..

with that being said, right now, I don't feel we need to handle error here, this is for notifying the upstream that connection state is changed, and upstream modules should take care of themselves.

Yeah so originally I wanted to use the .using method directly but ran into some problems. There are times when we need to add new native resources to the same scope of the parent but there's no guarantee that the parent is actually in an existing scope.

When this happens it leads to a few issues. First, the parent is in None scope which we cannot pass to ResourceScope.using. Second, we still want to execute the body and allocate all the new resources but don't want to want to make a new ResourceScope because all those new resources will disappear with the new scope.

Alternatives that I thought of were: 1.) to have the caller check whether or not it was in a scope and handle it appropriately. This is ugly and puts the onus on the callers in multiple places. 2.) Changing the using method to work with a None scope. I opted not to do this because it complicates that method and I believe would require changing the method parameters which I didn't want to do. 3.) Changing the default scope to be something other than None. This is probably a reasonable solution. Maybe we have some kind of base scope or something similar. That's likely to be a fairly significant change in both the design and behavior of this class.

`total_capacity_` is not necessarily initialized as an integer - it may be initialized as a double when a `ResourceIds` object is [instantiated with fractional resources](https://github.com/romilbhardwaj/ray/blob/ce30cdbc1238d21787a0dba17504d09c93ebdc7a/src/ray/raylet/scheduling_resources.cc#L238-L248). 

I think the cast to int is required in `int64_t capacity_delta = new_capacity - (int64_t)total_capacity_;` for correct behavior. Consider the two possible cases:
1) `total_capacity_` is a whole number -> there are no fractional resources. The cast has no effect.
2) `total_capacity_` is not a whole number -> there exist fractional resources. If we do not do the explicit `int64_t` cast, the subtraction `new_capacity - total_capacity_` followed by a `int64_t` cast for `capacity_delta` would result in an incorrect result. For instance, consider new_capacity = 8 and total_capacity_ = 7.2. The value of `capacity_delta` after evaluating `int64_t capacity_delta = new_capacity - (int64_t)total_capacity_;` would be 0, whereas we would want it to be 1 (and thus the resultant capacity would be 8.2).

I think so. The test is intended to ensure that kernels are properly unloaded if an exception is raised by user code within the context manager, not due to initialization problems with the context manager. Thus:
- `assert` in line 126 ensures that the kernel's database is initially empty.
- `assert` in line 132 ensures that local kernels were properly loaded, and it is not expected to fail.
- `assert False` simulates an exception raised by user code.
- `assert` in line 136 ensures that kernels are unloaded if an exception is raised, which is the point of the test.

The second assertion is a way to ensure that the kernel loading process has nothing to do with the next exception.

That being said, I've realized that, in the way I've written it, the test would also pass if the second assertion failed. I'll replace `assert False` for `raise RuntimeError` and change the argument of `pytest.raises()`.

`self.assertEqual()` doesn't work for two DataFrames. The `assertAllEqual()` and `assertAllClose()` from the `tf.test.TestCase` we're using doesn't work very well either. They succeed for equal/close DataFrames. But the big problem is that they don't check content of the indices. E.g., if you have two DataFrames with the same values and wall_times, but different run names, they won't catch the different. They'll just pass happily. Also, for failure conditions, they don't actually print out useful message about which column or which index differs between the two.

DataFrame has the `equals()` method. But it's not designed for unit testing. When two DataFrames are unequal, code like `self.assertEqual(dataframe.equals(expected))` won't tell you where the difference is.

The code here is a little long, but when failures occur, the line numbers gives you a clear idea which part of the DataFrame is the problem.

```python
import torch
```

Let me separate this into two separate points, because I think your last message is very-much on-target.

> Specifically for the case with timer where you have exactly 3 values I would lean slightly towards continuing to support the CSV mode there.

Yes, and I agree that with exactly 3 known columns, CSV is a nice format. However...

> Do you feel like if the CSV behavior exists for Timer but not elsewhere that it would be prohibitively weird?

Maybe sub "weird" for "creates surprising results", but yes. 😄 

Let's suppose that `globus transfer $src $dest --batch foo.txt` works today.
I'd like for `globus timer create transfer $src $dest --batch foo.txt --interval 10m ...` to also work, and vice versa. [1]

That means that because `globus transfer --batch` has its existing line-parsing behavior, we should support it in `globus timer create transfer`.

The "out" for this, if we much prefer the CSV usage, is to support both. And if we're going to do the work for that, I think we need `globus transfer` to support the same. If we implement both parsing styles, we need an option or usage to set the behavior. We could have `--batchmode [auto|txt|csv]` where `auto` has fancy behavior.

But all in all, this seems like it's going down a path of a new and separate feature to support CSV as a format for transfer batches. I'm not sure I'm convinced that the (IMO, mild) niceness of CSV for the simple case is worth it vs the cost of supporting it in both commands?

----

[1] I'm aware that this could be messed up by `--external-checksum`, but IMO that's a reason to push adding that to the AP and making it work, not to change how the CLI tries to make these commands appear related.

In ACMEv1 drafts -01 and -02, request bodies sent by ACME clients [sometimes needed to contain the `resource` field](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/draft-ietf-acme-acme-02#section-6.3). For challenges, there was the `type` field.

From what I can glean, Certbot was still doing this in ACMEv2 days until it was eventually fixed in #7176.

What I think is going on with this test, is that it was originally written according for the ACMEv1 -01 -02 drafts: the `resource` field is expected when `UpdateRegistration` is serialized to JSON.

Since in this PR, ACMEv1 support is gone, the serialization of an empty `UpdateRegistration` is now an empty JSON object.

Now that I have written out this novel, I realize that maybe we should look into whether we can just eliminate `ResourceMixin`, `TypeMixin` and `type` and `resource` fields in the relevant classes. If so, we should include them in the list of new deprecations in 1.30.0.

This is to accommodate differences in module naming between different providers.

The Terraform-derived providers use this format for member tokens:
```
pkg:namespace/member:member
```

The k8s provider uses this format:
```
pkg:namespace:member
```

The latter format is the format I'd like to standardize on, but doing so would require aliasing the type portion of every TF-derived resource. The regex-based approach allows us to avoid that, which seems nice.

Yep, `stdin=PIPE`. I’ve just finished a patch for this; it works great.

Not sure what you mean by keylogger-like? With `stdin=PIPE`, a new pipe
is created, and it’s never connected to a tty or other input source. We
just use it to send a single bit of metadata—the “close” signal.

In other words, from the _subprocess’s_ perspective it’s stdin, but it’s
not the _user’s_ stdin.

Is this about privacy? If TensorBoard wanted to log your keystrokes, we
could just do that, with no need for cloak and dagger.

Opening a pipe with input stream other than 0 (stdin) is harder and less
portable, which is why I’ve avoided it. Reading from it on the Rust side
requires using `unsafe`, since you’re taking an arbitrary int and
asserting that it corresponds to an fd over which you have exclusive
ownership, which there is no way to verify. And on the Python side you
need, like, `close_fds=False`, and you probably need to think more about
what Windows means by “file handle”… it seems more complicated, and
I don’t see a real benefit from it.

I am certainly open to alternatives. My criteria are (a) portable
without too much special casing, (b) doesn’t require special testing on
Windows, and (c) avoids `unsafe` in Rust if possible. I think this
approach does well on (a) and (c) and does okay on (b)—ideally I would
like to test this, but it’s simple enough that I’m crossing my fingers
that it just works.

Here's my suggestion for how we sequence these things:

```
frameworks_equal, installed_version = check_installed_version('tensorflow', tf.__version__)
try:
    MPI_LIB = _load_library('mpi_lib' + get_ext_suffix())
except Exception as e:
    check_installed_version('tensorflow', tf.__version__, e)
    raise e
else:
    check_installed_version('tensorflow', tf.__version__)
```

So basically we have 4 cases:

1. Exception was raised and version mismatch -> raise a HorovodVersionMismatchError
2. Exception was raised but versions equal -> re-raise original exception
3. No exception raised and version mismatch -> issue HorovodVersionMismatchWarning
4. No exception raised but versions equal -> do nothing

Not quite. `*` is a kind of special value, which is used **without** quotes. So if someone somehow will use `"*"` instead of `*`, it will be considered as regular etag.

Unfortunatly `ETag(value='*')` produced from `"*"` is indistinguishable from one produced from `*`.
So, for example:
```
"*", "tag", "other-tag" -> (ETag(value='*'), ETag(value='other-tag'))
* -> (ETag(value='*'), )
```
Maybe it's better to create separate type `ETAG_ANY` and change `Optional[Tuple[ETag, ...]]` to `Optional[Union[ETAG_ANY, Tuple[ETag, ...]]]`
```
"*", "tag", "other-tag" -> (ETag(value='*'), ETag(value='other-tag'))
* -> ETAG_ANY
```

We seem to use `@param[in]` in a lot of other similar situations where things in a `aMessage` (or in general an object) can change by the method/function.  Examples in the code:
- `Mle::AppendHeader(Message &aMessage)`, `AppendMode()`, and a bunch more in `Mle`,
- In `Tlv` class we have a lot of `Tlv::Append(Message &aMessage)` flavors,
- `Dataset::AppendMleDatasetTlv(Message &aMessage)`,
- `MessageQueue::Enqueue()/Dequeue()` also refer to `aMessage` as `@param[in]` (though they technically change some state in the `aMessage` object).

For consistency, I'd prefer to keep it the same in this PR.

----

We can discuss whether it makes sense to change all other occurrences (from a different PR probably):

Here is my preference on this:
- I would say `@param[out]` would be good when variable/object is changed fully (e.g., a new instance of object is created/used or object is modified completely).
- For `Append()` like of methods I would prefer to continue to use `@param[in]` over `[inout]` (if we go for `[inout]` the situations for methods like `Enqueue()/Dequeue()` is harder to consider).

Yes this creates a _global_ experiment that is shared by all trials. This needs to happen once in the driver process. Then, in each training process, we connect to the experiment that has already been created, and create a new run that represents just a single trial. So after we do `tune.run`, there is a single experiment containing multiple runs, with each run corresponding to a single tune trial. 

Now one potential approach is in _each_ training process, connect to the experiment if it already exists, and if not create a new one with the given name. However, this leads to race conditions (I tried it out and it didn't work). Instead, the user should have to create the experiment once in the driver.

I kinda feel your pain but here's my personal perspective on it.

There's a difference between *event loop based async I/O* in general and `async` / `await` as a pattern for consumption of the former. I feel your pain with `async` / `await` as a pattern but I don't think there is a way to get around *event loop based async I/O* in general.

I think event loop based async I/O is the best way to build heavy I/O dependent software like this especially in a language like Python. Because what else could we do?

1. If we just go synchronous it basically means all I/O is going to run sequentially -> slow

2. Threading out doesn't help much because of the GIL.

Hence, I think that the pattern of event loop based async I/O is fine by itself.

It is the pattern that browsers (as well as nodejs) use since forever and pretty much every browser API (apart from `prompt` which really locks the entire screen) is async because everything else will ruin the user experience. If you write blocking code in the browser, the browser will tell you that "this script is "hanging" and asks you to kill it".

However, **how** we deal with event loop based async I/O is a different story and for most of my life Futures (or Promises for that matter) as well as Observables have been my goto  answer to deal with it. Now I'm slowly adapting to `async` / `await` and part of me likes it and part of me doesn't like it.

However, in a language such as Python working with Futures doesn't feel very welcoming either (`future.add_done_callback(some_fn_that_I_wish_I_could_write_as_a_lambda_but_couldnt`). Here's how i think the implementation should have been done:
1. Introduce log_time field into the DB
2. Whenever an event is being stored, we set the timestamp field with the current time in ms.
3. When deserializing, we set the `log_time` field of those events.
4. an API call to fetch internal events will call [this function](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/master/raiden/api/rest.py#L825) which will make sure each event returned will have the field injected.

I find it easier for the users to use the advanced converters, because the core discord.py's converters are advanced. The users are used to call a function like that:
```py
await commands.MemberConverter.convert(ctx, text)
```
Using the basic converters would give this:
```py
commands.timedelta_converter(text)
```
Using a different style is quite a mess, especially since **`redbot.core.commands` inherits from `discord.ext.commands`** and include their converters. We should just keep the same style as the other converters. Just imagine:
- For a member, I call `commands.MemberConverter.convert()`
- For a channel, I call `commands.TextChannelConverter.convert()`
- For a role, I call `commands.RoleConverter.convert()`
- For time, I call `commands.timedelta_converter()`

That doesn't match at all with the other converters and will confuse the users. That's why I'm using advanced converters, so it works the same as the other ones, which are often used.

Basic converters may be useful for a personnal cog, but not for an API that already uses an other type of converters under the same module!

When looking at the discussion why this was introduced is that following code will work:
```python
import django_filters
django_filters.rest_framework.DjangoFilterBackend()
```

Instead if this import mechanism were not there following would need to be done to use it:
```python
import django_filters.rest_framework
django_filters.rest_framework.DjangoFilterBackend()
# or
from django_filters import rest_framework
rest_framework.DjangoFilterBackend
```

This is an API design decision by django filters that one import statement is enough to auto import all modules. We at this point do not have such an auto import mechanism in DJA and I would prefer to leave it this way.

Overall Django Filters still has `rest_framework` specific classes in its own module. As outlined above we should do the same as this way it is clear by the code that in this directory a dependency is needed to be installed.

And as we do not have the same API decision as django filters we also do not need `pkgutil`.

I believe the sparse and dense data structures here are just different representations in memory of the exact same matrices, and that specialized methods for them in LightGBM are just intended to allow that sparse data to stay sparse throughout training + scoring.

And I believe that's not directly related to the handling of missing data (which is described in more detail in the discussion at https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/issues/2921#issuecomment-607215320 and at https://lightgbm.readthedocs.io/en/latest/Advanced-Topics.html?highlight=missing#missing-value-handle).

Consider the following example:

```r
library(lightgbm)
library(Matrix)

set.seed(708L)

data("EuStockMarkets")

stockDF <- as.data.frame(EuStockMarkets)
feature_names <- c("SMI", "CAC", "FTSE")
target_name <- "DAX"

# randomly set a portion of each feature to NA or 0
for (col_name in feature_names) {
    stockDF[
        sample(
            x = seq_len(nrow(stockDF))
            , size = as.integer(0.01 * nrow(stockDF))
            , replace = FALSE
        )
        , col_name
    ] <- NA_real_
    stockDF[
        sample(
            x = seq_len(nrow(stockDF))
            , size = as.integer(0.01 * nrow(stockDF))
            , replace = FALSE
        )
        , col_name
    ] <- 0.0
}

X_mat <- data.matrix(stockDF[, feature_names])
y <- stockDF[[target_name]]
X_dgCMatrix <- as(X_mat, "dgCMatrix")

bst_mat <- lightgbm::lightgbm(
    data = X_mat
    , label = y
    , objective = "regression"
    , nrounds = 10L
)

bst_dgCMatrix <- lightgbm::lightgbm(
    data = X_dgCMatrix
    , label = y
    , objective = "regression"
    , nrounds = 10L
)


# predicted values don't depend on input type from training time or the type of newdata
preds_mat_mat <- predict(bst_mat, X_mat)
preds_dgCMatrix_mat <- predict(bst_mat, X_dgCMatrix)
preds_mat_dgCMatrix <- predict(bst_dgCMatrix, X_mat)
preds_dgCMatrix_dgCMatrix <- predict(bst_dgCMatrix, X_dgCMatrix)

stopifnot(
    all(
        all(preds_mat_mat == preds_dgCMatrix_mat)
        , all(preds_dgCMatrix_mat == preds_mat_dgCMatrix)
        , all(preds_mat_dgCMatrix == preds_dgCMatrix_dgCMatrix)
    )
)
```

If you find a case where this is not true and LightGBM is creating different predictions for sparse and, I'd consider that a bug worth addressing.

The size of the request is important, because it determines how quickly the script can run, and how much data it's pulling and processing at once. 

Initially, this default value was 60 minutes, but I was always overriding to 1440 minutes via my config file to speed up the polling of my configured TAXII server (I was using otx.alienvault.com). The user collections available to me to poll usually had data added every day, but not every hour. If I used the default value of 60 minutes, I was making 24 calls to download data when just 1 call would have achieved similar/same results.

I found a similar scenario with open.taxiistand.com, with data added usually daily and not hourly. The script took considerably longer to run when I didn't override the default value to 1440 minutes. 

A counter scenario could be a paid TAXII service, which may be updated more frequently than open source, free TAXII servers. In this case, a smaller `request_size_in_minutes` would be appropriate, to reduce the amount of content downloaded with each call. 

I can confirm scenarios where 1440 minutes is most helpful, but only assume a default of 60 minutes would be helpful. Therefore, I chose 1440 minutes

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/10932297/99890890-2e769b80-2c21-11eb-98a1-1be4622f6601.png)


Standard Frame CAN 2.0A
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/10932297/99890972-1bb09680-2c22-11eb-9dd7-1b5dab6b79f5.png)


Field name | Length (bits) | Purpose
-- | -- | --
Start-of-frame | 1 | Denotes the start of frame transmission
11 bit Identifier | 11 | A (unique) identifier which also represents the message priority
Remote transmission request (RTR)| 1 | Must be dominant (0) for data frames and recessive (1) for remote request frames 
Identifier extension bit (IDE) | 1 | Must be dominant (0) for base frame format with 11-bit identifiers
Reserved bit (r0) | 1 | Reserved bit.  Must be dominant (0), but accepted as either dominant or recessive.
Data length code (DLC) | 4 | Number of bytes of data (0–8 bytes)[a]
Data field| 0–64 (0-8 bytes) | Data to be transmitted (length in bytes dictated by DLC field)
CRC | 15 | Cyclic redundancy check
CRC delimiter | 1 | Must be recessive (1)
ACK slot | 1 | Transmitter sends recessive (1) and any receiver can assert a dominant (0)
ACK delimiter | 1 | Must be recessive (1)
End-of-frame (EOF) | 7 | Must be recessive (1)


Extended Frame CAN 2.0B
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/10932297/99890969-12bfc500-2c22-11eb-9c6a-1720455eb259.png)

Field name | Length (bits) | Purpose
-- | -- | --
Start-of-frame | 1 | Denotes the start of frame transmission
11 bit Identifier | 11 | First part of the (unique) identifier which also represents the message priority
Substitute remote request (SRR) | 1 | Must be recessive (1)
Identifier extension bit (IDE) | 1 | Must be recessive (1) for extended frame format with 29-bit identifiers
18 bit Identifier | 18 | Second part of the (unique) identifier which also represents the message priority
Remote transmission request (RTR) | 1 | Must be dominant (0) for data frames and recessive (1) for remote request frames
Reserved bits (r1, r0) | 2 | Reserved bits which must be set dominant (0), but accepted as either dominant or recessive
Data length code (DLC) | 4 | Number of bytes of data (0–8 bytes)[a]
Data field | 0–64 (0-8 bytes) | Data to be transmitted (length dictated by DLC field)
CRC | 15 | Cyclic redundancy check
CRC delimiter | 1 | Must be recessive (1)
ACK slot | 1 | Transmitter sends recessive (1) and any receiver can assert a dominant (0)
ACK delimiter | 1 | Must be recessive (1)
End-of-frame (EOF) | 7 | Must be recessive (1)

I added this b/c I am creating `Output`s from `Promise`s and returning them directly. In the original code, we would do something like

```
return new Output(promise).apply(v => output(v));
```

Now we do this:

```
return new Output(promise);
```

In the first case, the `apply` would ensure that if the promise resolved to an output, the inner output would be lifted onto the outer output. In the second case, there is no `apply`, so the `Output` constructor itself must handle the lifting. Strictly speaking, the approach I have here is "more correct" than the original code, as outputs will now never resolve to other outputs. Which I now realize is a breaking change, and which may end up _increasing_ our allocaitons-per-output. So maybe the original approach is a better choice--thoughts?

On the one hand, I think we should avoid to have separate APIs, at least for `internal` and `extacc` modes. This is because these modes are expected to be swappable:

- Define `mymode` in a *global* package.
- Set `mymode := internal` for a regular VUnit simulation (`id` is ignored) with no VHPIDIRECT support at all. Simulate with e.g. ModelSim.
- Without modifying a single additional VHDL line, set `mymode := extacc`. Now, simulating with GHDL, all the strings are accesible externally and inspectable at runtime.

Note that this is true for any new_string_ptr or new_byte_vector_ptr defined in any component/module of the design. The hierarchy is ignored and there is no need to traverse all the entities, because `id` values are absolute. Of course, care needs to be taken, since multiple ptrs with the same id will access the same shared buffer.

On the other hand, I agree that the current implementation of `length` is not clean enough. Precisely, `storage_t` contains a `length` field because:

- `type extstring_access_t is access string(1 to integer'high);`. It is not possible to define `type extstring_access_t is access string;`, since unconstrained arrays are handled as *fast pointers* by GHDL and *that's looking for problems* (see ghdl/ghdl#797).
- As a result, attribute `length` on strings of type `extacc` will always return `integer'high`, independently of the size that was allocated in C. In most use cases, the required size will be far smaller, so we use `length` when the string_ptr is created to let VUnit know the actual limit of that array.
  - As commented above, it is possible to have these arrays resized/reallocated by either GHDL or C. But a mechanism needs to be implemented in order to update the corresponding `length` field in the other part.
  - Furthermore, the length of each of the buffers in the C part is currently defined through custom variables. I.e., there is no structured approach, such as an array of integers that contains the actual length of each of the buffers in the array of pointers. This can also be implemented.
- With strings of type `extfnc` the point is that no shared pointer exists. All the interactions are done through foreign functioncs.
- With strings of type `internal`, field `length` of type `storage_t` is useless (`0`).

Re 1.: Given that the historical/current definition of retention is something like "a ping received in the third week after enrolling" - yes we do need arbitrary analysis windows.

> this could still produce a timeseries-style limits object with a second constructor, right?

Yes, we _could_ add an `analysis_window_start` column to the `enrollments` dataframe even when not querying a time series of data and thus avoid the `if time_limits.time_series_period is None` branch in `_get_join_conditions()`. Though `TimeLimits` would still need to know about `analysis_window_start` or we'd need to find another way to pass that info into `_process_enrollments()`, which would be messy. So the gross improvement would be the removal of a branch condition and the potential removal of the attribute `TimeLimits.analysis_window_end` (though I'd like to keep it, for validation)

> One-element dict is my favorite of these two (consistency!)

If "the standard format" accepted by the stats functions remains "a DataFrame with one row per client and one column with branch name and one column with metric data", then we should return data in the standard format wherever possible, so that it as easy as possible to use the stats functions (no searching around for your keys in the single metric case!). If we change "the standard format" to be a _dictionary_ of such dataframes and update the stats functions accordingly then it would make sense to return a one-element dict - though this of course has the downside of making it inconvenient to run computationally expensive stats for just a single point of the time series (I elaborate more on this argument below)

> I suggested elsewhere that maybe the return value of get_time_series_data() should be a single spark dataframe in tidy format with (client_id, analysis_window) ID columns, which I also like.

I think I said verbally that I consider this a footgun, because the stats functions assume that there is exactly one row per client, and have no way of checking whether this is true; it would be very easy for a DS to forget to filter the DataFrame to a single analysis window before feeding it to the stats functions. Alternatively we could  modify "the standard format" to include an analysis window column, and modify the stats functions to iterate over the sets of rows with shared analysis windows? But I think this is a bad direction: when possible we should prefer to define a function `X -> Y` then map a list over it to obtain `[X] -> [Y]` when needed, rather than commit to a direction where the simplest function we can define is `[X] -> [Y]`? > Would it make sense for our `build-support` code to be a separate resolve? This would I think resolve the issue we've had in the past of pantsbuild.pants code accidentally using build-support requirements, thanks to the visibility change we made recently.

Only if it needs to be. The theory behind separate resolves is that having a single resolve of user code is better for compatibility, because it eases sharing of code. It also reduces the number of permutations and unique requirements that are in use: in the presence of range requirements, it's possible for each different resolve to end up with a different item from the range. when all requirements are considered together, the total number of permutations is reduced, and one version is chosen (again, if it can be), which should improve performance and the amount downloaded.

So: you shouldn't create more resolves unless you have to because they contain incompatible requirements.

The tag must never be `None`. Indeed, run-level data, including graphs
and hyperparameters, does not have an obviously canonical embedding into
this model. The way that the `hparams` plugin does it is to use [fixed
tag names that are not `None`][hp], rather than fixed tag names that are
`None`.

Permitting `None` would allow _one_ extra value, at the cost of
complicating the typing significantly (especially in Python(…), but also
in database layers). Requiring plugins to use their own namespaces, like

```
__run_graph__
__conceptual_graph__/USER_PROVIDED_TAG
__profile_graph__/USER_PROVIDED_TAG
```

doesn’t introduce extra typing complexity and also has better semantics.

[hp]: https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorboard/blob/5a5023f96059caabd048f2687604b06d5ee8fa8f/tensorboard/plugins/hparams/metadata.py#L30-L32

The problem with current definition is that upper layer cannot get the complete Timestamp values. For example, Thread spec requires the MeshCoP mDNS Service to advertise the full Timestamp in the TXT data (keyed by `at`). If the user match the the Active Timestamp in mDNS service against the Active Dataset cache on mobile to test if the Active Dataset is up-to-date, it will always get a false result. It also force a Border Router implementation to violates the Thread spec (or do we want to change the spec?).

Do we want to consider the extra _O(log n)_ time complexity of querying the datastore's n-item B+-tree in this calculation?  As long as we're using sqlite as the underlying datastore, all loads/stores are at least _Omega(n log n)_ space and time.  However, the algorithms and data structures in SIP-004 do not require this -- an on-disk hash map will also work (which would not have this extra complexity).  I'm leaning towards designing the cost analysis to assume that the underlying datastore has the theoretically-best runtime (i.e. _O(1)_ load/store with an on-disk hashmap), but it will also mean that we'll need to drop sqlite at some point in the future.

This is indeed a big and complex subject, but I think we need to all agree on the how,when and what of error propagation before sending our users down any specific error handling path.

E.g., a common ask is to have a way for an application to quickly know if its broker list, security settings, are erroneously configured, ALL_BROKERS_DOWN is suitable for this, but only at startup.
IF an application has been running successfully for 3 weeks and suddenly gets an ALL_BROKERS_DOWN it is probably not due to configuration error.

- Should `len` or `__len__` be used?
- Can `__len__` accidentally consume an iterable? 
- Is `try: len(...)/except...` better? It seems more Pythonic and doesn't kill static analysis.
- Is there a better name than `iterable` which indicates it needs to have a length? It'd be surprising to pass e.g. a generator and see it fail, just based on the name.

I fully agree that using `eval` is an absolute no-go for untrusted sources. But here we are in full control of the file `version.txt`, so it's a "dangerous"  as putting any code into `__init__.py`, `version.py` or anywhere else.

Parsing the file, means to sort out all kinds of nasty problems, such as interpreting numbers correctly and stripping whitespace. And I'm not sure if that step is optimized properly, so it could be that it will be performed each time one loads any submodule from `post_office`.

Other proposal would be to use [ast.literal_eval](https://docs.python.org/3/library/ast.html#ast.literal_eval):

> Safely evaluate an expression node or a string containing a Python literal or container display. The string or node provided may only consist of the following Python literal structures: strings, bytes, numbers, tuples, lists, dicts, sets, booleans, and None.
This can be used for safely evaluating strings containing Python values from untrusted sources without the need to parse the values oneself. It is not capable of evaluating arbitrarily complex expressions, for example involving operators or indexing.

Let me break down just how tricky the single type thing in TorchScript is here (*it's been bugging me for days*). For instance, if I write a private standalone function _cast_fillcolor(), it must be called from `forward()` since only then would we know the image is tensor, but fillcolor would have to be the input for that function, which won't work since fillcolor is multi-type and anything called from `forward()` is casted in TorchScript thus needed to be single type.
The only way to support a multi-type variable is putting it as a class attribute, as in `self.xxx`. So, the only solution probably is implementing _cast_fillcolor() as a private class method so we can use self.fillcolor, and I use that new base class to avoid writing the same method for all 3 classes.

When a task completes, the Taskcluster-GitHub service can optionally include task-specific information in the check run displayed in the GitHub checks tab.  To do so, it looks for an artifact on the task named `public/github/customCheckRunText.md` and uses the artifact content as the check run text.  The artifact name can be customized by setting `task.extra.github.customCheckRun.textArtifactName` in the task definition.  For example:
```
payload:
  ...
  artifacts:
    public/github/customCheckRunText.md:
      type: file
      path: checkrun.md
```
or, if for example all build results are in `public/results`:
```
payload:
  ...
  artifacts:
    public/results:
      type: directory
      path: results
task:
  extra:
    github:
      customCheckRun:
        textArtifactName: public/results/checkrun.md
```

这段代码是用于压缩图像的函数。以下是我对代码进行简要审查的建议:

1. 在注释部分，建议将函数的参数和返回值进行说明，以提高代码可读性。

2. 第4行代码中的注释标签"@@ -100,7 +100,7 @@" 似乎是一个误差，并不属于Python代码，因此可以删除。

3. 第7行代码中的`max_size`参数建议进行类型检查，确保它是整数或浮点数。

4. 第9行代码中使用了`Image.LANCZOS`作为图像缩略算法，这是一种更高质量的方法，相比于默认的`Image.ANTIALIAS`算法会产生更好的结果。

5. 第11行代码中的`quality`变量转换为整数后，可以通过条件判断来进行合法性检查。

6. 异常抛出时的错误信息可以更加详细和友好，例如指定异常类型并提供具体的错误描述。

除了上述建议外，如果有可能的话，建议在函数开始处添加输入参数的类型注释和函数返回值的类型注释，这将有助于提高代码的可维护性和可读性。

If we leave this contex manager in place, we should move conditional import into this context manager, thus we will try to import colorama on each call of `ic`. And `import` statement is a costly opertaion.

I've tried to move context manager into another module (`windows_support`), to be able to import `colorama` on a module level. But in that case we have to use conditional function definition such as:
```python

if sys.platform == 'win32':
    from .windows_support import supportTerminalColorsInWindows

    def colorizedStderrPrint(s):
        colored = colorize(s)
        with supportTerminalColorsInWindows():
            stderrPrint(colored)
else:
    def colorizedStderrPrint(s):
        colored = colorize(s)
        stderrPrint(colored)
```

This Pipfile is only used for the test setup, and does not influence what packages are considered compatible when actually installing the compiler package itself. That said, when adding a requirement here, you should:
- Consider what versions of the dependencies we'd like to support. For example, we generally want to support the version that was latest at the time of writing, and any minor/patch releases around it, but not a major version bump (first number) since that might contain breaking changes.
- Then, if the package is required when using the compiler, and not just while testing it, you should make sure that the `setup.py` of the compiler package has the correct requirement that you have decided on. If the package is only used in tests, skip this step.
- Finally, you should add the package to the Pipfile, in the `dev` section if only used in tests, and in the non-dev section otherwise, and make sure the version bound matches what you decided earlier.

I also gotta say that although I do find it useful to name these things in the way that says yeah, it's abstract, I have not found metaclasses to be as useful as I expected them to be. Almost every time I've found a use for metaclasses, there's a just as easy way to make it with regular classes. (Actually, in Python I often use decorators.) Part of the issue is that the uses invariably thwart static analysis, so I end up finding static analysis more important than the other stuff. Maybe such tools would understand this class as a special case because it might have widespread use, but in general metaclasses just make it hard to understand.  - they specified a deprecated field

There is a very tense decision for the client to make - warning or error. Continuing without errors is going to cause havoc. But if the user sees an error, they would hope that no changes were committed. This is the closest we can get to some form of atomicity. Erring seems right to me. A warning would set the expectation that the object still exists, but would allow execution to continue. All options are messy, this is the best I could come up with.

I'm not sure we can handle it this way.
Example:

```python
class Order():
    customer = ForeignKey()

class Customer():
    preferences = ManyToManyField()


class Preference()
    name = CharField()
```
Let's say we have request `GET api/orders/?include=customer__preferences`, so it should produce queryset like `Order.objects.select_related(customer__preferences)` which will fail, because `preference` should be FK but not m2m relation.
And we can't do `select_related` and pass custom queryset like this:
Order.objects.select_related(Select('customer', queryset=Customer.objects.prefetch_related('preferences')))

So, `prefetch_related` covers _all_ cases. For everything else use `PreloadIncludesMixin`

The stopping point in any large-scale refactoring is somewhat arbitrary, and this is no exception; there's lots of other places where we were using the term "entity" in an ambiguous way, and it may take a little while before we're using the terms in a completely consistent manner.

In many places we also need to improve encapsulation or move responsibilities around, for instance in `test_runner` we have this:
```Python
if simulation.populations.get(key):
```
where what we really mean is:
```Python
if simulation.entity_exists(key):
```

It's easy to see that the implementation of `entity_exists` is near-trivial, but in the absence of this encapsulation the population/entity ambiguity has "leaked" more than it should have. (Also, as is now obvious, we should be asking the TaxBenefitSystem, not the Simulation.)

1. added comments and expanded docstring

3. *type annotations gave me some trouble*; since I had decided **not to change 'Path' to introduce Pattern**. I felt that doing it would have too wide impact.
  - added type GlobExtend which extends Glob by adding Pattern
  - made _split_path `_split_path(path: GlobExtend,...)` to allow for Patterns and Globs.
  - added test to avoid trying to apply lstrip to ints or bools, main benefit raising ValueError with a meaningful message. In some sense this checks that requirement in the type annotation for arg. path is met.
  - allowed Pattern as a leaf, this has the advantage of treating similarly `dpath.search(x, "{regex}" ...)`and `dpath.search(x, re.compile("regex"),...)` (and `dpath.search(x, [ re.compile("regex")],...)` which is the list form with length 1)
  
This way, the type annotation corresponds to what the function expects, and I hope everything is clearer.

I think the current implementation is correct, but I'm concerned that we're going about this from the wrong side. I'm not sure it makes sense to set a "default" retroactively like this. It feels like we're building up this list of predicates based off of the serializer's current behavior and finding the hole where we should put the default JSON body. This feels like a hard pattern to maintain because now you have to update the serializer's logic, but also make sure the assumptions being made in this finalization step still hold.

Can we not handle this more proactively before / during serialization? My hunch is that we should be able to set this payload/content-type up front and then have the serializer override the body/content-type if it knows better. This seems preferential to having the serializer implement some logic `A` around the model and then have this post serialization step where we check for `not A`.

> The individual simplify methods are popping from a list that we're iterating over, that's never a good idea.

Why not? It's much faster because iterating in Python is really slow. Like, it's faster in ~5 times because every simplifier is just if-else statements that gives no effect on speed, while new iteration over `self.parsed` takes the same time, as current `Motd.simplified()` call.

Yes, it would not be so important, if count that MOTDs are limited to ~500 symbols but 5 times difference is still huge.

> Even if we're iterating over a copy here, that might actually cause even more issues, as the `enumeration` that these functions receive is simply the index number on that copy, however the moment we pop out any value, all of the indices change.

Hooks don't have access to original `parsed` list, and **must not** save any info about previous elements. They must use `parsed[enumeration - 1]` to not break anything.

And the only method, that has access to original `parsed` list is `Motd.simplified`, which also must not depend on enumeration.

> Say we're at index 5 that's popped, now we go to index 6 in a copy, but in the original, where we popped from, index 5 was removed, and now became index 6, which means index 6 is now actually index 7, etc. Its' just really messy.

It's impossible if we will follow rules that I mentioned upper.

> it's much less weird than having a class like `MotdSimplifiers` that's basically just a namespace for a bunch of simplification methods.

Hi @ajkavanagh 

Sorry, it's been a busy 2 weeks, and I had to review my own code and choices here. :)

So, the code is _not exactly_ doing that; let me try to clarify.
The complexity comes from sticking to no behavior changes. :/

- We need that `finally:` block, because in the original code `if not ip_addr:` runs inconditionally, not just in case of unhandled exception in `ns_query()` (it can return `None` for `NXDOMAIN`).

- On the `fallback` check/return before `reraise`: good point.
If `fallback` is set, we should not `reraise`, but use that instead.

- Regarding `six`, I've used it to keep the backtraces in py2 consistent or at least useful; py3 has exception chaining, but IIRC in py2 (which doesn't) if we just `raise` the original exception, we lose the backtrace altogether; bad enough for debugging.
And then while I was trying to use a python exception thing one way or another, it would be a syntax failure or other problem in either python version.
So I eventually gave up on syntatic sugar and chose `six` (with this verbose `t, c, tb = exc_info()` approach.)

Hope this helps!

As I noted below, this PR currently isn't testing anything other than `BIDSLayout` initialization, and one consequence of allowing a `Path` as `root` is that any downstream code that accesses `.root` has to be able to handle that, which is not at all guaranteed—and which we can't easily verify in any case. Thanks for your reply. My current use-case is recording the video of a D415 camera (color stream at 1920 x 1080, 30 Hz; both infrared streams at 1280 x 720, 30 Hz) on a Raspberry PI 4B (2 GB) to an SSD attached via USB. I implemented my own recording code such that I have control over the file format and can directly write some additional information into it (such as frame timestamps that are synchronized with other devices over a network).

Writing the video data to the SSD seems to cause significant CPU load according to `htop`, even after switching from `fwrite()` to the `write()` syscall. Because of this, there is unfortunately a risk of frame drops. Initially, I tried to use the V4L2 backend, but it dropped many frames during recording. Implementing some optimizations (such as #9139, or this PR) reduced the amount of dropped frames, but did not eliminate frame drops. I then switched to the RSUSB backend which generally seemed to work better, but I also observed some (rare) frame drops with it.

So, the need for optimizations such as using the Y8I format comes from the risk of frame drops in the presence of high CPU load (e.g., due to recording as described above) and possibly load spikes (e.g., due to background processes waking up). For this, I think that it makes sense to avoid any unnecessary data processing.

Regarding CPU/memory-intensiveness, the de-interleaving step can possibly be compared to an "unoptimized memcpy of the whole frame with worse memory locality":
* "unoptimized", because it does not make use of platform-specific intrinsics (e.g., armv8 intrinsics on the Raspberry Pi 4), unless the compiler happens to be able to auto-vectorize the code.
* "worse memory locality", because the data is copied to two separate buffers instead of to a single location as in standard memcpy.

So, yes, under usual conditions the Raspberry PI 4 is more than fast enough to do processing steps like this, but it is the load spikes that I am concerned about, since I sometimes observe frame drops whose occurrence I would like to minimize.

Let me add the flow of calls:
- `IsSafeToHandleNow()` is only called from `HandleNotification(RxFrameBuffer)`.
- `HandleNotification(RxFrameBuffer)` is called from `HandleSpinelFrame()`
- This  itself is called from `HdlcInterface::Callbacks::HandleReceivedFrame()`
- The HDLC callback can be invoked when HDLC decoder `mHdlcDecoder` finishes decoding a full frame.
- This can only happen when `mHdlcDecoder.Decode()` is called and right before this we set `mIsDecoding` to `true`.

Thanks for the helpful feedback. 

>     1. If possible, not having this artificial reference would be easier for a user,

I agree. Unfortunately HDF5 does not a Null value and NAN is not defined for integer types. In the file itself, we therefore unfortunately can't get around storing the data with -1 values. That being said, replacing the -1 values on read with None (i.e, when slicing into the table or when converting it Pandas) should be doable. I'll take a look at this next week. 

>     2. Another confusion comes from the fact that, if either the `start_index` or the `index_count` is -1

I think this is similar to the issue you raised in 1. I think what we need to do is: 1) update the slicing and conversion to pandas for the ``TimeSeriesReferenceVectorData`` to convert entries that have -1 as start_index to None values, 2) update the docs of ``TimeSeriesReferenceVectorData`` to describe this behavior. The -1 value is here again used as a way to indicate None. The reason we need this here, is because our stimulus and response columns must align.

For the  ``add_recording`` function, I think we may also want to change the default value of start_index and index_count parameters to None.  The ``add_recording`` function calls ``__compute_index`` to determine the correct start_index and index_count for the given TimeSeries to use. I.e., if, e.g., not stimulus is given then both ``start_index`` and ``index_count`` will be automatically set to -1. Similarly, if a TimeSeries is provided for a stimulus and ``start_index`` and ``index_count`` were set to -1 then they will be converted by ``__compute_index`` to the appropriate values for the given TimeSeries, i.e, start_index will be set to 0 and the index_count to the length of the TimeSeries. 

>     But this relation is never (?) enforced

It is enforced when calling ``add_recording`` but it should also be enforced in ``TimeSeriesReferenceVectorData`` and the docs should be updated to mention this as well.

OK I done a bit of digging.  I **think** the `d and` bit is so we can handle masked arrays, and went in at #68.  If we have a masked array of datetimes

```python
unit = cf_units.Unit('days since 1970-01-01')
dates = unit.num2date(np.ma.masked_array(range(5), mask=[0, 0, 1, 1, 0]))
```

If we just do

```python
[d.replace(microsecond=0) for d in dates]
```
we get an `AttributeError`.  If instead we do

```python
[d and d.replace(microsecond=0) for d in dates]
```
we get our masked array back.
```
[cftime.DatetimeGregorian(1970, 1, 1, 0, 0, 0, 0, has_year_zero=False),
 cftime.DatetimeGregorian(1970, 1, 2, 0, 0, 0, 0, has_year_zero=False),
 masked,
 masked,
 cftime.DatetimeGregorian(1970, 1, 5, 0, 0, 0, 0, has_year_zero=False)]
```

Which is different from using `if d`, as that reduces the size of the array

```python
[d.replace(microsecond=0) for d in dates if d]
```
```
[cftime.DatetimeGregorian(1970, 1, 1, 0, 0, 0, 0, has_year_zero=False),
 cftime.DatetimeGregorian(1970, 1, 2, 0, 0, 0, 0, has_year_zero=False),
 cftime.DatetimeGregorian(1970, 1, 5, 0, 0, 0, 0, has_year_zero=False)]
```

So I **think** we should still be using `dt and`.

Normally, I'd try nowadays to add this additional behaviour as a Mixin (to favour composition over inheritance), e.g. like [here](https://stackoverflow.com/a/39471532), but it makes the correct inheritance formulation more fragile for our instrument contributors (correct order, correct use of `super()`), and I currently don't expect that we need more classes in the inheritance tree.

However, *if* we go towards these "reuseable, common-functionality" instrument classes (SignalGenerator and such) like you proposed in #416, this will get more complicated to deal with cleanly. Suddenly there is another axis to consider - to we make `SignalGenerator` *and* `SCPISignalGenerator` (to me this feels quite unnecessarily complicated)? 
As the scpi command set can be considered a feature/trait, maybe it would be better to use an `SCPIMixin`, after all, and any concrete implementation would then be `class KeithleyXYZ(SCPIMixin, SignalGenerator)` or `class RSXYZ(SignalGenerator)`? We then have to educate contributors on the importance of `super()`, order of base classes/MRO, and `*args, **kwargs` pass-through in initialisers. Probably this can best be sorted out with good Instrument templates/documentation.

It could be surprising but an empty input name is valid actually, though not very meaningful in practice... Empty input names are cases that the extracted model is using _constant inputs_ i.e. initializers. @askhade 

The example below is a `Transpose` node with constant input, and we are extracting the model itself - as it has only one node.
```py
import onnx
from onnx import helper, TensorProto

# generate original ONNX model
M, N, K = 2, 3, 4

wdata = [i for i in range(N*K)]
weight = helper.make_tensor('weight', TensorProto.FLOAT, [N, K], wdata)

output = helper.make_tensor_value_info('output', TensorProto.FLOAT, [K, N])

transpose = helper.make_node('Transpose', ['weight'], ['output'], name='transpose', perm=(1, 0))

graph = helper.make_graph([transpose, ], 'demo', [], [output], [weight])
opset = helper.make_operatorsetid(onnx.defs.ONNX_DOMAIN, 9)
model = helper.make_model(graph, producer_name='toy-demo', ir_version=6, opset_imports=[opset])

onnx.checker.check_model(model)
onnx.save_model(model, 'example.onnx')

# extract model
onnx.utils.extract_model('example.onnx', 'extracted.onnx', list(), ['output'])
```

> Does this provider refer to the component provider?

No, it's the provider where the `Call` is implemented. This is the same as with `Invoke`. When making an `Invoke` or `Call`, you call it on the monitor, and you can include the provider/version to use:

https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi/blob/398fb2f8523e9d5c4fa0a064aaeab7ecdd70ee82/sdk/proto/resource.proto#L26

And the engine will lookup the specified provider (otherwise using the default provider) and forward the call:

https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi/blob/398fb2f8523e9d5c4fa0a064aaeab7ecdd70ee82/pkg/resource/deploy/source_eval.go#L559-L561

https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi/blob/398fb2f8523e9d5c4fa0a064aaeab7ecdd70ee82/sdk/proto/provider.proto#L37

> How does this interact with the provider map that is supplied on construct? How does a call impl retrieve that?

It's unrelated. There's not currently a way to fetch a component's map of providers outside of when the component is being registered. If there are particular providers that you want to use when creating resources from methods, you'll either have to have the component save them in the state, or pass them in as arguments.

> Along the same lines, do resources created within call auto-parent to the component? Should this be configurable?

No, there's no auto-parenting.

> Do we need to provide ways to expose all resource properties to call?

I'm not sure what you mean. For methods, there is a `__self__` argument that is a resource reference to the component, so when it is deserialized, it will have all of the component's state available for use.

```python
def _dig_first(*pairs: Tuple[Mapping[str, Any], str], ignore_empty: bool = False) -> Setting:
    """
    Return the first dict item that matches from pairs of dicts and keys.
    Will throw a KeyError if missing.

    _dig_first((dict1, "key1"), (dict2, "key2"), ...)
    """
    if not pairs:
        raise ValueError('pairs cannot be empty')

    for dict_like, key in pairs:
        if key in dict_like:
            value = dict_like[key]

            if ignore_empty and value == "":
                continue

            return value

    raise KeyError(key)  # type: ignore
```

I don't think we should do b due to our current release process.

In order for us to push a correct tag for a release right now, we need to have already done the majority of the 1+ hour manual release process. This is largely because the tag includes an updated version of certbot-auto for the release which is signed and includes the hashes of our built Python packages for the release.

Before doing this manual process, I think we should run our full test suite on the closest thing to the code we want to release as we can. Not doing this can result in problems that would have been caught before manually building the release being caught afterwards and we have to repeat the process. I'm not OK with adding this potential headache for Erica and I to the release process.

With our current release script, the closest thing to the code we want to release for point releases is are *.x branches. We could trigger the builds manually before a release, but I think we are all trying to automate things more and not less so I don't think this is a good approach either.

For the other options, I don't have that strong of a preference, but I will say that in general:

1. I would like to keep Mattermost notifications for *.x branch failures because in most cases it's signalling test failures on the code we're preparing to release. I'd like Azure to make noise about that.
2. Between splitting pipelines and adding conditions, there is of course a balance to be struck, but when we're talking about adding a single condition, I personally prefer that over splitting pipelines. I think that results in fewer config lines and duplication and I think it is easier to read and understand our test setup rather than having it all spread over multiple files.

I'll also add option d which would be to skip the deploy stage in `advanced.yml` for `*.x` branches (or if it's not triggered by cron). This may mean undoing the change to make a reusable deploy stage.

This is the downside of having a `Partition` object vs a string key as the base API.  The partition set only knows how to produce an ordered list of `Partition` objects.  It does not know how to produce a `Partition` object for a string key.  The decorated function returns a set of tags for some generic partition, so we need to know how to resolve it.

It's actually really annoying to deal with generic `Partition` objects.  We have to deal with them for time window and dynamic based partition defs.  Also, since they might not be serializable, we have to resolve all the config/tags off of these definitions.

I'd actually be thrilled if we simplified the API and resolved everything to a string type, but I think that's a breaking change.

Maybe use bash array syntax here? Or else prefer `$*` over `$@` when trying to put all of the arguments into a single variable given how `$*` expands to a single Bash "word" when in double quotes.

See `man bash`:

```
    Special Parameters
       The  shell  treats  several parameters specially.  These parameters may only be referenced;
       assignment to them is not allowed.
       *      Expands to the positional parameters, starting from one.  When the expansion is  not
              within double quotes, each positional parameter expands to a separate word.  In con-
              texts where it is performed, those words are subject to further word  splitting  and
              pathname expansion.  When the expansion occurs within double quotes, it expands to a
              single word with the value of each parameter separated by the first character of the
              IFS  special  variable.   That is, "$*" is equivalent to "$1c$2c...", where c is the
              first character of the value of the IFS variable.  If IFS is unset,  the  parameters
              are  separated  by spaces.  If IFS is null, the parameters are joined without inter-
              vening separators.
       @      Expands to the positional parameters, starting from one.   In  contexts  where  word
              splitting  is  performed, this expands each positional parameter to a separate word;
              if not within double quotes, these words are subject to word splitting.  In contexts
              where word splitting is not performed, this expands to a single word with each posi-
              tional parameter separated by a space.  When  the  expansion  occurs  within  double
              quotes,  each  parameter expands to a separate word.  That is, "$@" is equivalent to
              "$1" "$2" ...  If the double-quoted expansion occurs within a word, the expansion of
              the  first parameter is joined with the beginning part of the original word, and the
              expansion of the last parameter is joined with the last part of the  original  word.
              When  there  are no positional parameters, "$@" and $@ expand to nothing (i.e., they
              are removed).
```

```
>>> from django.contrib.auth.models import User
>>> p1 = User(username='Bob')

>>> p3 = User(username='Steve')

[<User: Bob>, <User: John>, <User: Steve>]

for p in all_people:

(1, {'admin.LogEntry': 0, 'auth.User_groups': 0, 'auth.User_user_permissions': 0, 'auth.User': 1})

[<User: Bob>, <User: John>, <User: Steve>]

<QuerySet []>
```

If a known error is encountered, it outputs the respective error message. Otherwise it outputs a basic error message together with the raw error output of the VCS command.

```python
try:
    vcs.clone(repo_url, checkout, clone_to_dir, repo_dir)
except subprocess.CalledProcessError as clone_error:
    output = clone_error.output.decode('utf-8')
    if 'not found' in output.lower():
        raise RepositoryNotFound(
            'The repository {} could not be found, '
            'have you made a typo?'.format(repo_url)
        )
    if any(error in output for error in BRANCH_ERRORS):
        raise RepositoryCloneFailed(
            'The {} branch of repository {} could not found, '
            'have you made a typo?'.format(checkout, repo_url)
        )
    raise RepositoryCloneFailed(
        'Cloning of Repository {} returned an error:\n{}'.format(
            repo_url, output
        )
    )
```

This makes sense to me now. From a practical perspective I think you're right that we could probably ship this as-is and never run into an issue... but the topic piqued my interest, and I'm not sure if this theoretically weakens the hash at all.

If I wanted to be very safe, I would hash a tuple of the code and line number. That would guarantee the strength of the hash. But there is a performance penalty there. We probably don't want to construct tuples in the `python_trace_callback` loop.

A look at https://stackoverflow.com/questions/5889238/why-is-xor-the-default-way-to-combine-hashes provides some interesting insight.

From a theoretical perspective, the block hash should a fairly random "good hash". However, the line number - which we are effectively treating as a hash - won't be. According to the [hash_guidelines](https://www.javamex.com/tutorials/collections/hash_function_guidelines.shtml), we should spread the line number bits out a bit by multiplying them with some constant. 

If you feel fancy, I think we can afford a few more shifts and additions without adding too much overhead, so maybe we can do something like the "boost::hash_combine" mentioned in that post?

Please allow me to comment on the customization style for classes (very sorry for the length of my comment).

`PyMeasure` style has always encouraged use of properties to "customize" `Instrument` class to implement a real instrument.
In order to achieve that, a set of helper functions (static methods) has been added to help users to define properties in "controlled" way.
Also, when this results in something not generic enough, users have always the possibility of:
- define its own python properties
- define methods

This style, in my opinion, enforces a certain discipline in implementing new instrument, by using tools provided, but eventually other ways are accepted if the tools provided are not powerful enough to capture particular behavior.

In addition, it encourage to implement stuff at class level instead of instance level.

In this PR, I saw the possibility to address several use cases as mentioned by @LongnoseRob, @moritzj29 and also myself.
In a very synthetic way, we would like to address:
- Iteration of instrument functions (e.g. channels)
- Composition of instrument functions (e.g. multifunction instrument)

In the end, @bmoneke  is proposing to provide an `add_child` method which allow to capture the above behaviors.

I would like to re-propose what I initially proposed in terms of tools to define channels/instrument functions ("things") in an instrument. Essentially instrument's implementer have to:
1 Define classes for the "things"
2 Define class for the instrument
3 Define class attribute to link the "things" to the instrument

The first two points are now well defined, thanks to the work of @bmoneke.
For the 3rd point, I would like to propose alternative as previously mentioned in https://github.com/pymeasure/pymeasure/pull/718#issuecomment-1289632777.
Let me recap here for clarity:
```
class MyInstrument(Instrument):
    things= {
         'name1' : MyChannel,
         'name2' : MyChannel,
         'name' : (MyChannel,)*2,
         'func1' : MyInstrumentFunc1,
         'func2' : MyInstrumentFunc2,
    }
.
.
.
```
That is: implementer defines at class level an attribute that capture all the "things" defined in the instrument.
In `__init__` of `Instrument` class, we should loop over the "things" class data structure and call `add_child` as appropriate.

I think this method has several advantages:
- The loop in the `__init__` method should be present on all the instrument with "things", but here is centralized in Instrument.`__init__`
- A walk through algorithm over all "things" can be implemented at class level without the need to instantiate the instrument.
- channels are defined at class level, meaning that they are related to the instrument and not to the instrument instance (channel instances are defined during instrument instantiation)
- Use case for family of instruments with different number of "things" should be easier to manage:
```
class MyInstrument4ChannelModel(Instrument):
    things= {
         'analog_input' : (MyAnalogInput,)*4,
    }
.
.
.

class MyInstrument2ChannelModel(MyInstrument4ChannelModel):
    things= {
         'analog_input' : (MyAnalogInput,)*2,
    }

```
- Reuse of channels in different instrument should be easier
- when everything else fails, implementer should use `add_child` method directly or any variant of it.

I just wanted to draw your attention to a possible different style of presenting channel feature in `PyMeasure` and I acknowledge that some implementation details (which I hope are minor) are neglected in my explanation.

I am looking for the smallest float where the calculations are still useful/meaningful. The idea here is to have the smallest possible float where I can subtract a number of similar size without loosing precision, i.e. I want to be able to shift the exponent by the size of the precision. For instance, `1.02 - 1.01 = 1e-2`, but if `1e-1` is already the smallest, this would be `0.0`. Therefore, I divide the smallest non-zero float by the precision to add the number of significant digits to the exponent of the smallest non zero float.

The logic I design the test cases is divided into two situations. 
- The **dict** type is at last
- The **dict** type is not at the last (any position except the last). 

Pytorch onnx team has a function **_decide_input_format()** to process the input sample. 
- When the **dict** type is not at the last, we don't need to do anything additional, just feed the original input _sample.
- When encountering the situation that the last item in the input sample is a **dict** type, the function will treat it as a dictionary containing optional arguments and resolve it  (read the keys of the dictionary, check if it is among the arguments of the forward function. If yes, then append it to the input list. If no, do nothing when the arg has no default value). So if we simply leave the input of **dict** type at the last, it won't be put in the input list by onnx by default, so I did a small trick here.  

For reference, the **_decide_input_format()** is here. 
https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/main/torch/onnx/utils.py#:~:text=def%20_decide_input_format(model%2C%20args)%3A

Ok I think I found the issue:

```python
import gc
from typing import Any, Generic, TypeVar
from weakref import WeakValueDictionary

T = TypeVar("T")

class MyGeneric(Generic[T]):
    pass

cache: WeakValueDictionary[Any, Any] = WeakValueDictionary()

Concrete = MyGeneric[int]
cache[(MyGeneric, int)] = Concrete
del Concrete

gc.collect(0)
gc.collect(1)
gc.collect(2)

referrer = next(iter(gc.get_referrers(next(cache.values()))))
print(referrer)  # <functools._lru_cache_wrapper object at 0x102dee560>
print(referrer.__module__)  # typing
```

So it looks like this cache inside of `_tp_cache` of `typing.py` may be holding onto a reference.
It has a `maxsize` of 128, which basically means that the `WeakValueDict` will act as a regular dict for the _last_ 128 things that got run through it!

That explains why the test that creates thousands of objects and measures memory consumption looks like it's not accumulating but this one measuring just 1 reference does.

(I wonder if `npm` even has this timeout issue). I guess if we do ever get around to switching to `npm`, we could still keep this publicly exported variable, we just wouldn't be using it anymore, since we would be running `npm` rather than `yarn` (unless there's a need for a similar mutex for `npm` and we co-opt this mutex for that purpose as well).

Yeah, but the capability shouldn't be there anyway, IMO.  GCE itself doens' thave the capability to sign: it can get `access_tokens` and even an `id_token` but not sign arbitrary blobs by itself.  IMO the computeCredential object should just reflect its baseline capabilities the underlying provider gives here anyway (in this case, the provider is the metadata service).  

The `credentials.Signing` capability is baked into the existing class as really just a side effect  too interms of gettig an IDtoken:  if you were using the IAM api, you could 'just get' an id_token [directly](https://cloud.google.com/iam/credentials/reference/rest/v1/projects.serviceAccounts/generateIdToken)  without signing

Anyway, her'es the biggest problem i see,  that in the existing implementation, the singer and sign_bytes was already _exposed_  directly [here](https://github.com/googleapis/google-auth-library-python/blob/master/google/auth/compute_engine/credentials.py#L229)

```python
    def signer(self):
        return self._signer

    def sign_bytes(self, message):
        return self._signer.sign(message)
```

I don't think people are using that capability to direct sign via IAM but it is there.

---

What the existing implementation is doing is basically allowing the GCE to "impersonate itself" and use the IAM API to get an id token and along the way also allow blob signing.   I would say if someone wants to sigin via the IAM api, the `impersonated_credential` object could do that (i'll file a separate PR for something like [this](https://gist.github.com/salrashid123/5caaa6178e786bfb48515a2cfb7631af#file-impersonated_credentials-py-L242) later

This gets more complicated when we add DeepSpeed support, which automatically handles saving/loading the model object's state. If we have a regular model and a traced model, it tries to load parameters for both. The traced parameters don't exist, so we need additional logic to filter them out. With a single model instance, the `ModelWithLoss` object state can saved/loaded as a standard Pytorch module.

This also helps us minimize complexity. We have a `ModelWithLoss` object that turns input batches into loss outputs using the fully traced/wrapped model. We also have the "vanilla" `SockeyeModel` for saving parameters, running the checkpoint decoder, etc. This way the object with added complexity (`ModelWithLoss`) only needs to support a single use case.

>  In our discussion, @andrewsg pointed out many cases data is a string containing a JSON-encoded dict. 

I'm not sure where `data` is going to be a string in cases where `if_etag_in_json` is called.

A quick survey of usage:

- Outside of tests, `is_etag_in_json` is used only as the predicate for the `DEFAULT_RETRY_IF_ETAG_IN_JSON` retry policy:

```bash
$ git grep -B 1 is_etag_in_json google/
google/cloud/storage/retry.py-
google/cloud/storage/retry.py:def is_etag_in_json(data):
--
google/cloud/storage/retry.py-DEFAULT_RETRY_IF_ETAG_IN_JSON = ConditionalRetryPolicy(
google/cloud/storage/retry.py:    DEFAULT_RETRY, is_etag_in_json, ["data"]
```
The `DEFAULT_RETRY_IF_ETAG_IN_JSON` policy is used in exactly three places:

```bash
$ git grep DEFAULT_RETRY_IF_ETAG_IN_JSON google/ | grep -v import
google/cloud/storage/blob.py:        retry=DEFAULT_RETRY_IF_ETAG_IN_JSON,
google/cloud/storage/bucket.py:        retry=DEFAULT_RETRY_IF_ETAG_IN_JSON,
google/cloud/storage/hmac_key.py:    def update(self, timeout=_DEFAULT_TIMEOUT, retry=DEFAULT_RETRY_IF_ETAG_IN_JSON):
google/cloud/storage/retry.py:DEFAULT_RETRY_IF_ETAG_IN_JSON = ConditionalRetryPolicy(
```

which turn out to be:

- `Blob.set_iam_policy` (passes a `dict` to `client._put_resource`)
- `Bucket.set_iam_policy` (passes a `dict` to `client._put_resource`)
- `HMACKeyMetadata.update` (passes a `dict` to `client._put_resource`)

Although `Bucket.patch`, `Blob.patch`, `Bucket.update`, and `Blob.update` do not use the `DEFAULT_RETRY_IF_ETAG_IN_JSON` policy, they all pass `dict` to `client._patch_resource`.

We do expose `DEFAULT_RETRY_IF_ETAG_IN_JSON` as a public symbol in the public `retry` module, so I guess there could be cases out in the wild which use it for some other API calls, but I'm doubtful any such hypothetical users are actually getting real retries done.

We basically provide two ways to access items in the array
- We have `operator[]` which does not do any bound checks (up to caller to validate) and undefined behavior if index is out of bounds.
- `At()` which does bound checking (safer to use).

I tried to mirror these to be similar to what we have in `ot::Array()` class (which is fixed length array) and it is also similar to how `std::array` API works (`at()` does bound checking and `operator[]` does not).

In general, I prefer to avoid adding asserts in the simpler utility types (to keep them more flexible and smaller code size) and leave it to caller/user of the type to decide if they want to add checks.

-------

Regarding `Type` being pointer itself, I think it can work fine.

If `Type` itself is pointer then `At()` returns a "pointer to a pointer", and caller can tell the difference between pointer being `nullptr` (entry not found in the array) or `*ptr` being `nullptr` (the entry is in array but its value itself is `nullptr`).

If I remember it correctly, this test needs to showcase async pbt v.s. sync pbt. So it was originally set up to be:
async:
trial1 result --> trial2 result --> trial1 result --> perturb --> trial whatever result
sync:
trial1 result --> trial2 result --> trial1 result --> .. --> trial3 result --> perturb

The sequence is important to showcase the difference between sync and async. Specifically, trial1 should proceed to `iter=2` strictly before trial3 does.

Having `30` is giving race condition to the above sequence. This is because
trial1 iter=1 (ts = 10)--> pause --> trial2 iter=1 (ts = 20) --> (I) trial2 paused and trial1 is running again --> (II) trial3 iter=1 (ts = 30) --> (III) trial1 iter=2 (ts = 10 + 20 + epsilon)

Because of this change, step (I) needs to happen in the next tune loop (because pg is not cached and readily to be used). This small epsilon manifests the racing condition, that was originally there in the set up (30 = 10 + 20)

This PR isn't primarily intended to reduce collisions - more entropy into the filename is a side effect of the main purpose.

This PR is intended to make it easier for a human doing debugging to deal with "From other logs, it looks like something weird happened with workers in block 7. Let's go look if the batch system said anything about that block/job."

I think a better choice than `nplike = ak.nplike.of(where)` is `nplike = self.nplike`, which is to say, "regardless of whether the `where` array is on the CPU or GPU, move it to be on the same device as `self`." In fact, `where` could be a non-array, like a Python list, in which case `ak.nplike.of(where)` would return NumPy, but `self.nplike` could be CuPy. It will have to eventually be moved to the device `self` is on, so better to do that early.

Since this is only used once on the following line, `self.nplike` can just be used inline there.

Actually the code here and below seems to make some assumptions that are not guaranteed, namely:
- that the tarball is located in the current directory (can be easily fixed by a `os.path.basename()`)
- that the tarball contains a directory with the same name as the archive (minus the `.tar.gz`)
- that the tarball doesn't have files that will be created outside of the c.w.d. (see the warning in https://docs.python.org/3/library/tarfile.html#tarfile.TarFile.extractall )

I think we could at least verify that the tarball is "well-behaved" by calculating the common prefix directory of the members of the archive: if there is one, it can replace `extract_dir `here; otherwise, we just bail out.
